title: Observation of end-to-end pumping in a quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type photonic chain
authors: Arnob Kumar Ghosh, Ang Chen, Ashraf El Hassan, Patric Holmvall, Mohamed Bourennane, Annica M. Black-Schaffer
abstract: Topological pumps offer a promising route to operate as connecting buses, supplying efficient and robust connectivity between non-neighboring elements in a network. Here, we investigate a finite quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type photonic chain and demonstrate its ability for end-to-end pumping, with only small and simple changes to the system. First, we use a tight-binding formalism to numerically show that a localized pumping state can be transferred between opposite ends of the system, with only a small structural change to the chain. Then, we experimentally implement this topological pump in an array of coupled optical waveguides, where light propagation is effectively described by the tight-binding model under the paraxial approximation, enabling direct correspondence between theory and experiment. We numerically simulate and experimentally demonstrate pumping by injecting light into a single waveguide at one end of the setup, which activates a localized pumping state. As the light propagates along the wave guide array, it is also pumped to the other end. We further show that pumping remains robust against structural deformation, such as controlled defects in the waveguide array. Our results establish that quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type photonic lattices are a robust and experimentally viable platform for disorder-resilient state transfer.
summary: The researchers developed a simplified method to pump light from one end of a quasiperiodic photonic chain to the other by only adjusting two waveguides. This approach is highly robust against structural defects and avoids the need for complex, system-wide tuning. This discovery provides a viable platform for reliable state transfer in topological photonic networks.
main_problem: The need for an efficient and robust end-to-end pumping protocol in topological systems that does not require continuous, fine-tuned control of all bond strengths along the entire chain.
main_result: Demonstrated that a localized pumping state can be transferred between opposite ends of a quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type photonic chain by modulating only two outer bonds, a process that remains robust against structural defects.
method: The study uses a tight-binding formalism for numerical simulations and an experimental platform consisting of an array of coupled optical waveguides fabricated via femtosecond laser direct writing.
model_or_system: A 1D quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type photonic chain implemented in an array of 11 coupled optical waveguides, where light propagation is described by a tight-binding Hamiltonian with coupling strengths determined by waveguide spacing.
key_observables: Light intensity distribution at the output facet, eigenvalue spectra as a function of the phason angle, and the excitation gap between the pumping state and adjacent eigenstates.
important_parameters: Number of waveguides (N=11), characteristic distances (dA=10 um, dB=7 um), phason angle (phi), and coupling strength (J=63.5 mm^-1).
main_assumptions: The paraxial approximation is used to map Maxwell's equations to the Schrodinger equation, and waveguide diameters are assumed to be uniform to neglect onsite terms.
figures_summary: Fig 1 shows the waveguide array schematic and tight-binding representation; Fig 2 displays eigenvalue spectra and spatial distribution of the pumping state; Fig 4 shows the experimental setup; Fig 5 compares theory and experiment for light transfer; Fig 6 demonstrates robustness against structural defects.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the problem of efficient pumping, presents the numerical tight-binding model and the proposed simplified protocol, details the experimental waveguide fabrication and implementation, demonstrates successful end-to-end transfer, and concludes with tests of robustness against defects and future directions.
why_interesting: This work is relevant for quantum optics and many-body dynamics as it demonstrates a simplified, robust method for state transfer in quasiperiodic systems, which could serve as a blueprint for connecting non-neighboring elements in quantum networks or photonic circuits.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

figures.caption: FIG. 1. (a) Schematic representation of the waveguide array illustrating the end-to-end pumping of light in a quasiperiodic Fibonacci-type system. The inter-waveguide separation has two characteristic distances dA and dB at z = 0 and L. To pump the light, only two waveguides (green) are bent along the propagation direction (z). Light is then injected at z = 0 at the left end (red arrow), and the pumped light emerges at z = L from the opposite end of the array. (b) Tight-binding approximation of the waveguide array at z = 0 (configuration 1) and z = L (configuration 2), with weak bonds or coupling (JA, corresponding to larger separation dA) in blue and strong bonds (JB, corresponding to...

FIG. 2. (a) Eigenvalue spectra E (mm−1) of the Hamiltonian H [Eq. (2)] as a function of phason angle ϕ. Red curve marks the pumping state. (b) Eigenstate of the pumping state as a function of the waveguide index i for phason angles ϕ1 (green) and ϕ2 (orange).
FIG. 3. Evolution of the pumping state at z = 0, L/4, L/2, 3L/4, and L. Colorbar highlights the weight of the propagating pumping state |ψ(z)|2 at a given site. Here, L = 40 mm.

FIG. 4. Schematic of experimental setup. Light from a tun- able laser is focused by an objective to selectively excite indi- vidual waveguides in the array. The output light is collected by a second objective and imaged onto a CCD camera.

FIG. 5. Demonstration of end-to-end pumping of light in a waveguide array theoretically (top panel, similar to bottom panel in Fig. 3) and experimentally (bottom panel). Yellow circle indicates the injection point of light at z = 0. Colorbar encodes the light intensity at different waveguides at z = L.
